headline: Peat Fires Released 120 Million Tonnes of CO2 in 2024
context: CIFOR data showed Malaysian peat fires in 2024 released approximately 120 million tonnes of CO2 equivalent, concentrated in Sarawak and southern Johor peatlands.

cards.big: Malaysia's total annual emissions are 290 million tonnes of CO2.
cards.sub: Peat fires alone added another 120 million tonnes in 2024 — equivalent to 41% of normal national output.

cards.big: 82% of 2024 peat fires occurred on drained agricultural land.
cards.sub: Drainage canals built for oil palm lower the water table, making peat combustible. Rewetting is technically feasible.

cards.big: The National Peatland Action Plan 2011 targeted zero burning by 2020.
cards.sub: The target was missed. No revised timeline has been published.

cards.big: Transboundary haze cost ASEAN economies USD 5.7B in 2024.
cards.sub: Malaysia contributed an estimated 28% of regional haze but faces no penalties under the ASEAN Agreement.

cards.big: Peat fires are not natural disasters. They are consequences of drainage decisions made decades ago.
Rewetting peatlands is cheaper than fighting fires. The economic case is clear; what is missing is political willingness to restrict drainage on private land.
